instruction.p: In a physics experiment, students investigate the relationship between the volume of a gas and its pressure at a constant temperature (Boyle's Law).

table.class: table table-bordered table-clean text-center table-valign-middle table-striped table-condensed

problem-template.mathyon-s:
# --- Data Generation (n=6) --- # Use larger x-values so y-values stay in a smaller, student-friendly range. x1, x2, x3, x4, x5, x6 = randpick( (4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12), (4, 6, 8, 12, 16, 24), (5, 6, 8, 10, 15, 20) )
# Underlying Model: P = 120/V approx (with small noise) y1 = N(round(120/x1 + randint(-2, 2))) y2 = N(round(120/x2 + randint(-2, -1))) y3 = N(round(120/x3 + randint(-2, 2))) y4 = N(round(120/x4 + randint(-2, 2))) y5 = N(round(120/x5 + randint(1, 2))) y6 = N(round(120/x6 + randint(-1, 1)))
# --- SPT 1: Scatterplot Points --- P1 = plot.point(x1, y1) P2 = plot.point(x2, y2) P3 = plot.point(x3, y3) P4 = plot.point(x4, y4) P5 = plot.point(x5, y5) P6 = plot.point(x6, y6)
# Interactive points start at y=0 S1 = plot.point(x1, 0) S2 = plot.point(x2, 0) S3 = plot.point(x3, 0) S4 = plot.point(x4, 0) S5 = plot.point(x5, 0) S6 = plot.point(x6, 0)
# SPT 3: Transformation (1/x) # IMPORTANT: force FLOATS using 1.0 and 100.0, then round to 2 d.p. # This ensures the regression uses the same (rounded) 1/x values students enter.
valRx1 = (round(100*(1/x1))/100) valRx2 = (round(100*(1/x2))/100) valRx3 = (round(100*(1/x3))/100) valRx4 = (round(100*(1/x4))/100) valRx5 = (round(100*(1/x5))/100) valRx6 = (round(100*(1/x6))/100)
rx1 = fixeddec(valRx1, 2) rx2 = fixeddec(valRx2, 2) rx3 = fixeddec(valRx3, 2) rx4 = fixeddec(valRx4, 2) rx5 = fixeddec(valRx5, 2) rx6 = fixeddec(valRx6, 2)
# SPT 4 and 5: Regression Statistics (use rounded valRx values)
sumRx = N(valRx1 + valRx2 + valRx3 + valRx4 + valRx5 + valRx6) meanRx = N(sumRx / 6)
sumY = N(y1 + y2 + y3 + y4 + y5 + y6) meanY = N(sumY / 6)
sumSqXX = N( (valRx1-meanRx)^2 + (valRx2-meanRx)^2 + (valRx3-meanRx)^2 + (valRx4-meanRx)^2 + (valRx5-meanRx)^2 + (valRx6-meanRx)^2 )
sumSqYY = N( (y1-meanY)^2 + (y2-meanY)^2 + (y3-meanY)^2 + (y4-meanY)^2 + (y5-meanY)^2 + (y6-meanY)^2 )
sumSqXY = N( (valRx1-meanRx)*(y1-meanY) + (valRx2-meanRx)*(y2-meanY) + (valRx3-meanRx)*(y3-meanY) + (valRx4-meanRx)*(y4-meanY) + (valRx5-meanRx)*(y5-meanY) + (valRx6-meanRx)*(y6-meanY) )
rVal = N(sumSqXY / sqrt(sumSqXX * sumSqYY)) rDisp = fixeddec(rVal, 2)
slopeB = N(sumSqXY / sumSqXX) slopeBDisp = fixeddec(slopeB, 3)
intA = N(meanY - slopeB * meanRx) intADisp = fixeddec(intA, 3)
slopeB2 = fixeddec(rDisp*sqrt(sumSqYY)/sqrt(sumSqXX), 3) intA2 = fixeddec(meanY - slopeB2 * meanRx, 3)
slopeB3 = fixeddec(rVal*sqrt(sumSqYY)/sqrt(sumSqXX), 3) intA3 = fixeddec(meanY - slopeB3 * meanRx, 3)

instruction.p: Use technology to create a scatterplot of these data.

first-hint.p: Drag each point on the graph so that it matches the (Volume, Pressure) pairs from the table.

second-hint.p: The horizontal axis is Volume (x) and the vertical axis is Pressure (y).

instruction.p: Does the data set appear to be linear or non-linear?

first-hint.p: Do the data points appear to fall approximately along a straight line, or do they better fit some other curve or pattern?

second-hint.p: As volume increases, pressure decreases rapidly at first and then levels out.

instruction.p: Perform the transformation of the x-values by completing the last column of the table.
Round your answers to two decimal places.

first-hint.p: To find the transformed value, we calculate the reciprocal of the volume (x).

second-hint.p: For the first row, the volume is x1.
Calculate 1/x1 and round to two decimal places.
Do the same for the next rows.

instruction.p: Determine the coefficient of correlation of the transformed data set.
Round your answer to two decimal places.

second-hint.p: Enter the 1/x values as the independent variable list and the y values as the dependent variable list.

instruction.p: Determine the equation of the least-squares regression line for the transformed data set.
Give your answer in the form y = a + b \left(\frac{1}{x}\right), where a and b are rounded to three decimal place.

first-hint.p: Calculate the gradient b using technology.
Calculate the intercept a using the formula a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x}_{trans}, where \bar{y} is the mean of y and \bar{x}_{trans} is the mean of the transformed x values.

second-hint.p: Substitute the values of a and b into the equation form y = a + b \left(\frac{1}{x}\right).
